## DEEP GATED CANONICAL CORRELATION ANALYSIS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) models can extract informative correlated representations from multimodal unlabelled data. Despite their success, CCA models may break if the number of variables exceeds the number of samples. We propose Deep Gated-CCA, a method for learning correlated representations based on a sparse subset of variables from two observed modalities. The proposed procedure learns two non-linear transformations and simultaneously gates the input variables to identify a subset of most correlated variables. The nonlinear transformations are learned by training two neural networks to maximize a shared correlation loss defined based on their outputs. Gating is obtained by adding an approximate _ℓ_ 0 regularization term applied to the input variables. This approximation relies on a recently proposed continuous Gaussian based relaxation for Bernoulli variables which act as gates. We demonstrate the efficacy of the method using several synthetic and real examples. Most notably, the method outperforms other linear and non-linear CCA models.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) (Hotelling, 1936; Thompson, 2005), is a classic statistical method for finding the maximally correlated linear transformations of two modalities (or views). Using modalities _**X** ∈_ R _[D][x][×][N]_ and _**Y** ∈_ R _[D][Y][ ×][N]_ , which are centered and have _N_ samples with _Dx_ and _Dy_ features respectively. CCA seeks canonical vectors _**ai** ∈_ R _[D][X] ,_ and _**bi** ∈_ R _[D][Y] ,_ such that _,_ _**u** i_ = _**a[T] i[X]**[,]_[and] _**[ v][i]**_ **[=]** _**[b][T] i[Y]**[ , i]_[=][1] _[, ..., N]_[,][maximize][the][sample][correlations] between _**ui**_ and _**vi**_ , where _**ui**_ ( _**vi**_ ) form an orthonormal basis for _i_ = 1 _, ..., d_ , i.e.

While CCA enjoys a closed-form solution using a generalized eigen pair problem, it is restricted to the linear transformations _**A**_ = [ _**a**_ 1 _, ...,_ _**a** d_ ] and _**B**_ = [ _**b**_ 1 _, ...,_ _**b** d_ ].

In order to identify non-linear relations between input variables, several extensions of CCA have been proposed. Kernel methods such as Kernel CCA (Bach & Jordan, 2002), Non-paramatric CCA (Michaeli et al., 2016) or Multi-view Diffusion maps (Lindenbaum et al., 2020) learn the non-linear relations in reproducing Hilbert spaces. These methods have several shortcomings: they are limited to a designed kernel, they require _O_ ( _N_[2] ) computations for training, and they have poor interpolation and extrapolation capabilities. To overcome these limitations, Andrew et al. (2013) have proposed Deep CCA, to learn parametric non-linear transformations of the input modalities _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . The functions are learned by training two neural networks to maximize the total correlation between their outputs.

Linear and non-linear canonical correlation models have been widely used in the setting of unsupervised or semi-supervised learning. When _d_ is set to a dimension satisfying _d < Dx, Dy_ , these models find dimensional reduced representations that may be useful for clustering, classification or manifold learning in many applications. For example, in biology (Pimentel et al., 2018), neuroscience (Al-Shargie et al., 2017), medicine (Zhang et al., 2017), and engineering (Chen et al., 2017). One key limitations of these models is that they typically require more samples than features, i.e. _N > Dx, Dy_ . However, if we have more variable than samples, the estimation based on the closed form solution of the CCA problem (in Eq. 1) breaks (Suo et al., 2017). Moreover, in high dimensional data, often some of the variables are not informative and thus should be omitted

from the transformations. For these reasons, there has been a growing interest in studying sparse CCA models.

Sparse CCA (SCCA) (Waaijenborg et al., 2008; Hardoon & Shawe-Taylor, 2011; Suo et al., 2017) uses an _ℓ_ 1 penalty to encourage sparsity of the canonical vectors _**a** i_ and _**b** i_ . This can not only remove the degeneracy inherit to _N < Dx, Dy_ , but can improve interpetability and performance. One caveat of this approach is its high computational complexity, which can be reduced by replacing the orthonormality constraints on _**u** i_ and _**v** i_ with orthonormality constraints on _**a** i_ and _**b** i_ . This procedure is known as simplified-SCCA (Parkhomenko et al., 2009; Witten et al., 2009), which enjoys a closed form solution. There has been limited work on extending these models to sparse nonlinear CCA. Specifically, there are two kernel based extensions, two-stage kernel CCA (TSKCCA) by Yoshida et al. (2017) and SCCA based on Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (SCCAHSIC) by Uurtio et al. (2018). However, these models suffer from the same limitations as KCCA and are not scalable to a high dimensional regime.

This paper presents a sparse CCA model that can be optimized using standard deep learning methodologies. The method combines the differentiable loss presented in DCCA (Andrew et al., 2013) with an approximate _ℓ_ 0 regularization term designed to sparsify the input variables of both _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . Our regularization relies on a recently proposed Gaussian based continuous relaxation of Bernoulli random variables, termed gates (Yamada et al., 2020). The gates are applied to the input features to sparsify _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . The gates parameters are trained jointly via stochastic gradient decent to maximize the correlation between the representations of _**X**_ and _**Y**_ , while simultaneously selecting only the subsets of the most correlated input features. We apply the proposed method to synthetic data, and demonstrate that our method can improve the estimation of the canonical vectors compared with SCCA models. Then, we use the method to identify informative variable in multichannel noisy seismic data and show its advantage over other CCA models.

## 1.4 STOCHASTIC GATES

In the last few years, several methods have been proposed for incorporating discrete random variables into gradient based optimization methods. Towards this goal, continuous relaxations of discrete random variables such as (Maddison et al., 2016; Jang et al., 2017) have been proposed.

Such relaxations have been used in several applications, for example, model compression (Louizos et al., 2017), feature selection or for defining discrete activations (Jang et al., 2016). We focus on a Gaussian-based relaxation of Bernoulli variables, termed Stochastic Gates (STG) (Yamada et al., 2020), which were originally proposed for supervised feature selection. ## 2 DEEP GATED CCA

## 2.1 MODEL

It is appealing to try to combine ideas from Sparse CCA into the rich differentiable model of Deep CCA. However, a straight forward _ℓ_ 1 regularization of the input layer of a neural network does not work in practice because it makes the learning procedure unstable. This was observed in the supervised setting by Li et al. (2016); Feng & Simon (2017). This instability occurs because the objective is not differentiable everywhere. To overcome this limitation, we use the STG random variables (see Eq. 3) by multiplying them with the features of _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . Then, by penalizing for active gates using a regularization term E _∥_ **z** _∥_ 0, we can induce sparsity in the input variables.

We formulate the problem of sparse nonlinear CCA by regularizing a deep neural network with a correlation term. We introduce two random STG vectors into the input layers of two neural networks which are trained in tandem to maximize the total correlation. Denoting the random gating vectors **z** _x_ and **z** _y_ for view _**X**_ and _**Y**_ respectively, the Deep Gated CCA (DG-CCA) loss is defined by

where _**θ**_ = ( _**θX** ,_ _**θY**_ ), _**µ**_ = ( _**µX** ,_ _**µY**_ ) are the model parameters, and _λx_ , _λy_ are regularization parameters that control the sparsity of the input variables. The vectors **z** _x_ and **z** _y_ are random STG vectors, with elements defined based on Eq. 3.

Fig. 2 highlights the proposed architecture. Each observed modality is first passed through the gates. Then, the outputs of the gates are used as inputs to a view-specific neural sub-net. Finally, the shared loss term in Eq. 4 is minimized by optimizing the parameters of the gates and neural sub-nets.

## 3 RESULTS

In the following section we detail the evaluation of the proposed approach using synthetic and real datasets. We start with two linear examples, demonstrating the performance of DG-CCA when _N ≪ DX , DY_ . Then, we use noisy images from MNIST and seismic data measured using two channels to demonstrate that DG-CCA finds meaningful representations of data even in a noisy regime. For a full description of the training procedure as well as the baseline methods, we refer the reader to the Appendix.

## 3.2 MULTI VIEW SPINNING PUPPETS

As an illustrative example we use a dataset collected by Lederman & Talmon (2018) for multiview learning. The authors have generated two videos capturing rotations of 3 desk puppets. One camera captures two puppets, while the other captures another two, where one puppet is shared across cameras. A snapshot from both cameras appears in the top row of Fig. 4. All puppets are placed on a spinning device that rotates the dolls at different frequencies. In both video there is a shared

parameter, namely the rotation of the common bulldog. Even thought the Bulldog is captured from a slightly different angle, we attempt to use CCA to identify a linear transformation that projects the two Bulldogs in to a common embedding. We use a subset of the spinning puppets dataset, with 400 images from each camera. Each image has 240 _×_ 320 = 76800 pixels (using a gray scaled version of the colored image), therefore, there are more feature than samples and direct application of CCA would fail. We apply the proposed scheme using _λy_ = _λx_ = 50, a linear activation and embedding dimension _d_ = 2. DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . The active gates are presented in the bottom row of Fig. 4.

In Fig. 5 we present the coupled two dimensional embedding of both videos. Both embeddings are highly correlated with the angular orientation of the Bulldog. Note that adjacent images in the embedding are not necessarily adjacent in the original ambient space, this is because the Bunny and the Yoda puppets are gated and do not affect the embedding.

**Figure 4:** Top: two samples from the spinning puppets videos. Arrows indicate the spinning direction of each puppet. Bottom: the converged active gates for each video. There are 372 and 403 active gates for the left and right videos respectively.

## 3.3 NOISY MNIST

MNIST (LeCun et al., 2010) which consists of 28 _×_ 28 grayscale digit images, with 60K/10K images for training/testing, is a well known and widely investigated dataset. We use a challenging variant of MNIST with coupled noisy views. The first view is created by adding noise drawn uniformly from [0 _,_ 1] to all pixels. The second view is created by placing a random patch from a natural image in the background of the handwritten digits. Both views are based on different digit instances drawn from the same digit class. Random samples from both views are presented in Fig. 6. Both views consists of 62 _,_ 000 samples, of which we use 40 _,_ 000 for training 12 _,_ 000 for testing and 10 _,_ 000 are used as a validation set. Here the validation set is used for early stopping of the training procedure and optimizing _λ_ = _λx_ = _λy_ .

Multi-view processing of the two noisy views can generate an informative representation of the noisy MNIST data. In the following we will focus on performing unsupervised embedding of each noisy MNIST view into correlated 10 dimensional space.

By reducing the correlation cost, the DG-CCA learns which per-view pixels are relevant and informative in correlation maximization sense. In the bottom right corner of Fig 6 we present the location of the active gates. DG-CCA selects features (pixels) within an oval-like shape in the center of each view thus capturing the digit information and reducing the influence of the noise.

To measure the class separation in the learned embedding, we apply _k_ -means clustering to the stacked embedding of the two views. We run _k_ -means (with _k_ = 10) using 20 random initializations and record the run with the smallest sum of square distances from centroids. Given the cluster assignment, _k_ -means clustering accuracy (KM ACC) and mutual information (MI) are measured using the true labels. Additionally, we train a Linear-SVM (LSVM) model on our train and validation datasets. LSVM classification accuracy (LSVM ACC) is measured on the remaining test set. The performance of DG-CCA compared with several baselines appears in Table 2. In the appendix we provide all implementation details and provide an experiment demonstrating the performance for various values of _λ_ = _λx_ = _λy_ .

## 3.4 SEISMIC EVENT CLASSIFICATION

Next, we evaluate the method using a dataset studied by Lindenbaum et al. (2018). The data consists of 1609 seismic events. Here, we focus on 537 explosions which are categorized into 3 quarries. The events occurred between the years 2004 _−_ 2015, in the southern region of Israel and Jordan. Each event is recorded using two directional channels facing east (E) and north (N), these comprise the coupled views for the correlation analysis. Following the analysis by Lindenbaum et al. (2018), the input features are Sonogram representations of the seismic signal. Sonograms are time frequency

## 4 CONCLUSION

In this paper we present a method for learning sparse non-linear transformations which maximize the canonical correlations between two modalities. Our method is realized by gating the input layers of two neural networks which are trained to maximize their output’s total correlations. Input variables are gated using a regularization term which encourages sparsity. This allows us to learn informative representations even when the number of variables far exceeds the number of samples. Finally, we demonstrate that the method outperforms existing methods for linear and non-linear canonical correlation analysis.

## A APPENDIX

## A.1 GATES INITIALIZATION

The Gaussian based stochastic gates suggested by Yamada et al. (2020) are based on trainable parameters _**µ**_ and a constant parameter _σ_ . These control the mean and standard deviation of the injected noise respectively. Yamada et al. progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is technically well-motivated, addressing a clear gap in the literature: the lack of a scalable, sparse, non-linear CCA framework for high-dimensional regimes ($D > N$). The writing is generally professional, logically structured, and provides a necessary historical context that justifies the transition from classical CCA to Deep CCA and, finally, to the proposed "Deep Gated-CCA." However, the paper suffers from significant formatting and technical presentation issues that hinder immediate readability. The inclusion of raw, broken LaTeX strings (e.g., `_X_ ∈ R _[D][x][×][N]_`) makes the mathematical definitions difficult to follow, suggesting a lack of final-stage proofreading. Furthermore, while the motivation for using Gaussian-based continuous relaxations of Bernoulli gates is sound, the introduction of the method’s mechanics currently lacks sufficient depth; the jump from the objective function to the gating mechanism needs a more rigorous connection to the optimization landscape. To move forward, the authors must polish the technical typesetting to professional standards and ensure the "1.1 BACKGROUND" section, which is currently empty, is populated with a clear conceptual roadmap that bridges the gap between Deep CCA's backpropagation-based training and the discrete nature of the proposed gating variables.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important problem—learning sparse, non-linear canonical correlations from high-dimensional multimodal data—and proposes a reasonable combination of deep CCA with stochastic gating for approximate ℓ₀ regularization. The writing is generally understandable but suffers from numerous formatting errors, missing equations (e.g., Eq. 2 is never given), and inconsistent notation that detract from clarity and professionalism. The most significant issues are: (1) insufficient novelty, as the contribution appears to be a straightforward integration of existing methods (DCCA and stochastic gates) without clear theoretical or algorithmic innovation; (2) the method description in the provided excerpt is incomplete—how gates are applied, how the correlation loss and sparsity penalty are jointly optimized, and how the approach avoids trivial solutions or local optima are not explained; (3) the claim of outperforming other CCA models is unsubstantiated without specifying baselines, evaluation metrics, or experimental results, none of which are shown in the first 8000 characters; (4) the paper does not address potential issues with the gating mechanism in the high-dimensional regime (e.g., convergence, identifiability, or computational cost). Overall, while the problem is timely, the current presentation lacks the depth and rigor expected for a top-tier conference, and the contribution needs to be more clearly differentiated from prior work.

comments.title: Correlation formula in Figure 3 lacks normalization
comments.quote: ρ̂ = φ̂^T X^T Y η̂
comments.explanation: The empirical correlation between the estimated representations is given as a simple dot product of the projected data. However, the correlation is properly defined as (φ̂^T X^T Y η̂) / (‖φ̂^T X‖ ‖η̂^T Y‖) unless the projections have been normalized to unit variance. The text does not specify that such normalization is applied, and the DG-CCA loss does not explicitly enforce unit variance of the projections. A reader could mistakenly interpret the expression as the correlation itself, which is misleading. Given that the reported value matches the true correlation ρ=0.9, the authors likely computed the normalized measure, but the equation as written is technically incorrect.

comments.title: Garbled and likely incorrect expression for total correlation in Algorithm Details
comments.explanation: The OCR-produced string is unreadable, but the fragments suggest an attempted formula for the total correlation (sum of canonical correlations). Standard DCCA (Andrew et al., 2013) defines total correlation as the sum of the top d canonical correlations, which can be expressed as the trace of (Σ̂_XX^{-1/2} Σ̂_XY Σ̂_YY^{-1} Σ̂_YX Σ̂_XX^{-1/2})^{1/2}. The garbled text seems to involve Σ̂_YY^{-1/2}, Σ̂_YX, Σ̂_XX^{-1/2}, Σ̂_XY, and Σ̂_YY^{-1/2} in that order, which is not the standard symmetric form and likely would not yield the sum of correlations (it would give a different quantity, possibly the sum of squared correlations if a square root is missing). Even accounting for OCR, the ordering and the apparent omission of a matrix square root could mislead a reader who tries to implement the algorithm. The authors should provide a clear, correct formula consistent with the DCCA objective referenced elsewhere.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript tackles a timely and practically relevant problem by integrating continuous gate-based feature selection with Deep CCA, offering a promising solution to the well-known degeneracy of canonical correlation methods in high-dimensional ($p \gg n$) regimes. The motivation is clearly articulated, and the conceptual integration of differentiable gating with correlation-maximizing deep learning objectives is coherent and well-positioned within the literature. However, the presentation is hindered by inconsistent mathematical notation, fragmented equation formatting, and typographical errors that occasionally obscure core derivations and reduce overall clarity. Most significantly, the excerpt provides insufficient detail on the optimization dynamics of the gating mechanism; it remains unclear how the $\ell_0$-approximate gate parameters are stabilized during joint backpropagation (e.g., through straight-through estimators, temperature scheduling, or entropy/regularization terms), nor does it address potential failure modes such as premature gate collapse or vanishing gradients. Additionally, the introduction lacks a concrete description of evaluation baselines, computational complexity analysis, and theoretical or empirical safeguards that would contextualize the claimed performance improvements over linear and non-linear CCA variants. Clarifying the training procedure for the gates, ensuring consistent notation, and explicitly delineating the novelty relative to existing sparse non-linear CCA approaches will be essential for establishing the paper's methodological rigor, reproducibility, and conference

Given the symmetry of the CCA formulation, the second constraint should clearly reference the second modality: $\|\mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{Y}\|_2 < 1$. comments.title: Numerically impossible correlation value reported
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ .
comments.explanation: The text reports a correlation coefficient of 1.99. Standard correlation metrics (including Pearson and canonical correlations) are mathematically bounded within $[-1, 1]$. A value of 1.99 is strictly impossible for a properly normalized single correlation metric. Given the paragraph explicitly states the embedding dimension is $d=2$, this value almost certainly represents the *total correlation* (i.e., the sum of the $d=2$ canonical correlations, which approaches 2.0) or is a typographical error for 0.99. Without explicit clarification that a non-standard aggregated metric is being used, this claim misrepresents statistical bounds and requires correction or precise definition.

comments.title: Mismatch between prose description and Table 1 contents
comments.quote: In Table 1 we present the estimated correlation along with the estimation errors of _**φ**_ and _**ρ**_ (averaged over 100 simulations).
comments.explanation: The prose explicitly states that Table 1 contains estimation errors for $\phi$ and $\rho$. However, Table 1 lists columns for $\hat{\rho}$ (estimated correlation), $E_\phi$ (error for $\phi$), and $E_\eta$ (error for $\eta$). The scalar $\rho$ is the correlation coefficient itself and does not have a corresponding error column; the second error metric clearly corresponds to the canonical vector $\eta$. This is a direct contradiction likely stemming from a copy-paste error where $\rho$ was written instead of $\eta$. Readers cross-referencing the table will be misled.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'left part' of the loss function
comments.quote: Specifically, we used Monte Carlo sampling to estimate the left part of Eq. 4.
comments.explanation: Equation 4 defines the total loss as a sum of a correlation term and two regularization terms involving expectations over stochastic gates. The phrase 'left part' is vague. Context implies the authors mean the regularization term $\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{z}\|_0$, which requires MC sampling for its expectation, whereas the correlation term is computed empirically from the batch. The phrasing could mislead readers into thinking the correlation term requires stochastic estimation. Clarifying which specific component uses the single-sample MC estimator versus deterministic batch computation is necessary for reproducibility.

comments.title: Mathematical inaccuracy regarding $\ell_1$ regularization instability
comments.quote: However, a straight forward _ℓ_ 1 regularization of the input layer of a neural network does not work in practice because it makes the learning procedure unstable. This was observed in the supervised setting by Li et al. (2016); Feng & Simon (2017). This instability occurs because the objective is not differentiable everywhere.
comments.explanation: The authors justify avoiding $\ell_1$ regularization by claiming it causes instability due to non-differentiability. However, the $\ell_1$ norm is convex and differentiable almost everywhere; its only non-differentiable point is at zero. The cited literature refers to high-dimensional solution instability or variance in feature selection, not a lack of differentiability. The authors conflate subgradient discontinuity at zero (or $\ell_0$ behavior) with the cited optimization instability. While avoiding $\ell_1$ on input layers may be valid practical advice, the mathematical justification is inaccurate and should be corrected to reference gradient oscillation, poor conditioning, or sparsity-induced instability.

comments.title: Inconsistent canonical vector symbol in Table 1 caption
comments.quote: **Table 1:** Evaluating the estimation quality of the canonical vectors _**ψ**_ and _**η**_ .
comments.explanation: The caption for Table 1 refers to the canonical vectors $\psi$ and $\eta$, but the paper consistently uses $\phi$ and $\eta$ (e.g., Section 3.1, table columns $E_\phi$). The symbol $\psi$ appears to be a typographical slip or OCR artifact. It should be corrected to $\phi$ to match the manuscript and avoid confusion with standard notation where $\psi$ often denotes a different vector or activation function.

comments.title: Typographical error in Appendix initialization procedure
comments.quote: where _**u**_ **¯** and _**u**_ **¯** are the thresholded versions of the absolute value of the singular vectors.
comments.explanation: In the gate initialization description, the text incorrectly repeats $\bar{\mathbf{u}}$ twice. Given that the preceding steps compute the leading left and right singular vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ of the cross-covariance matrix $\mathbf{S}_{XY}$, the second term should logically be $\bar{\mathbf{v}}$. This is a minor typographical oversight that should be corrected for algorithmic clarity.

comments.title: Severely garbled covariance regularization description
comments.quote: where the matrix] [ γ] _**[I]**_[(] _[γ]_ _[>]_[ 0] _**[X]**_[) is added to] and _**Y**_ as stabilize the invertability of **Σ**[] _X_ and **Σ**[] _Y_ .
comments.explanation: The paragraph describing the regularization of empirical covariance matrices is heavily corrupted by PDF extraction artifacts. While the standard intent is to add $\gamma \mathbf{I}$ (where $\gamma > 0$) to the diagonal of $\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_X$ and $\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_Y$ to ensure invertibility, the current syntax is broken and difficult to parse. Rewriting this sentence cleanly is recommended to prevent confusion regarding hyperparameter definitions and improve reproducibility.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript tackles a timely and practically relevant problem by integrating continuous gate-based feature selection with Deep CCA, offering a promising solution to the well-known degeneracy of canonical correlation methods in high-dimensional ($p \gg n$) regimes. The motivation is clearly articulated, and the conceptual integration of differentiable gating with correlation-maximizing deep learning objectives is coherent and well-positioned within the literature. However, the presentation is hindered by inconsistent mathematical notation, fragmented equation formatting, and typographical errors that occasionally obscure core derivations and reduce overall clarity. Most significantly, the excerpt provides insufficient detail on the optimization dynamics of the gating mechanism; it remains unclear how the $\ell_0$-approximate gate parameters are stabilized during joint backpropagation (e.g., through straight-through estimators, temperature scheduling, or entropy/regularization terms), nor does it address potential failure modes such as premature gate collapse or vanishing gradients. Additionally, the introduction lacks a concrete description of evaluation baselines, computational complexity analysis, and theoretical or empirical safeguards that would contextualize the claimed performance improvements over linear and non-linear CCA variants. Clarifying the training procedure for the gates, ensuring consistent notation, and explicitly delineating the novelty relative to existing sparse non-linear CCA approaches will be essential for establishing the paper's methodological rigor, reproducibility, and conference

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for canonical vector modality
comments.quote: Note that the relaxed inequality constrain on _**a[T] X**_ and _**b[T] Y**_ makes the problem bi-convex, however, if _∥_ _**a[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1 or _∥_ _**b[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1, then the covariance in the objective is no longer equal to the correlation.
comments.explanation: Reading through Section 1.3, the authors consistently use _**a**_ for the canonical vector of modality X and _**b**_ for modality Y. In the quoted sentence, the second norm constraint is written as _∥_ _**b[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1, which incorrectly attaches the vector _**b**_ to X. Given the parallel structure of CCA and the earlier mention of _**b[T] Y**_, this is almost certainly a typographical oversight for _**b[T] Y**_. While a knowledgeable reader would easily correct it, the inconsistency momentarily breaks the formal notation flow and could confuse those verifying the bi-convexity conditions.

comments.title: Numerical impossibility of reported correlation value
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ .
comments.explanation: The authors report a correlation of 1.99 for the extracted embedding. Standard correlation coefficients (including Pearson and CCA canonical correlations) are mathematically bounded within [-1, 1]. A value of 1.99 cannot represent a single correlation metric. The authors likely either intended to report the sum of the `d=2` canonical correlations (which would approach 2.0) or made a typo (e.g., 0.99). Without explicit clarification that a non-standard aggregated metric is being used, this claim misrepresents the statistical bound and requires correction or precise definition to avoid misleading readers about the model's performance.

comments.title: Mismatch between prose description and Table 1 contents
comments.quote: In Table 1 we present the estimated correlation along with the estimation errors of _**φ**_ and _**ρ**_ (averaged over 100 simulations).
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that Table 1 contains estimation errors for _**φ**_ and _**ρ**_. However, examining Table 1 reveals columns for `ˆ_ρ_` (estimated correlation), `E_**_φ_**` (error for vector _**φ**_), and `E_**_η_**` (error for vector _**η**_). The scalar `_**ρ**_` is already reported as `ˆ_ρ_` and does not have an error column; the second error metric in the table clearly corresponds to `_**η**_`, not `_**ρ**_`. This is a direct contradiction between the narrative and the data presentation, likely stemming from a copy-paste error or typo where `_**ρ**_` was written instead of `_**η**_`. A careful reader relying on the table for the reported error values would be misled.

comments.title: Incorrect modality in sparsity constraint condition
comments.quote: if _∥_ _**a[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1 or _∥_ _**b[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1, then the covariance in the objective is no longer equal to the correlation.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly established earlier in Section 1.3 that variables $a$ and $b$ correspond to modalities $X$ and $Y$, respectively. Here, the condition mistakenly references $\|b^T X\|_2$ instead of $\|b^T Y\|_2$. Since $b$ weights features from the $Y$ modality, the norm must be taken with respect to $Y$. This is a clear notation inconsistency/typo that could confuse readers about which covariance matrix is being discussed.

comments.title: Mathematical inaccuracy regarding $\ell_1$ differentiability
comments.quote: However, a straight forward _ℓ_ 1 regularization of the input layer of a neural network does not work in practice because it makes the learning procedure unstable. This was observed in the supervised setting by Li et al. (2016); Feng & Simon (2017). This instability occurs because the objective is not differentiable everywhere.
comments.explanation: The authors justify their use of Stochastic Gates by claiming $\ell_1$ regularization causes instability due to non-differentiability. However, the $\ell_1$ norm is convex and differentiable almost everywhere; its only non-differentiable point is at zero. The instability cited in the referenced literature (e.g., Feng & Simon, 2017 on Lasso stability) refers to high-dimensional solution instability or variance in feature selection, not to a lack of differentiability in the objective function. The authors appear to conflate subgradient discontinuity at zero with the cited instability, and with the $\ell_0$ norm (which is indeed discontinuous). While the practical recommendation to avoid $\ell_1$ on input layers is valid, the mathematical justification provided is inaccurate and should be corrected to reflect gradient oscillation or poor conditioning rather than non-differentiability.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'left part' of the loss function
comments.quote: Specifically, we used Monte Carlo sampling to estimate the left part of Eq. 4.
comments.explanation: Equation 4 defines the total loss as a sum of a correlation term and two regularization terms involving expectations over stochastic gates. The phrase 'left part' is vague. Based on context, the authors likely mean the regularization term $\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{z}\|_0$, which requires MC sampling for its expectation, whereas the correlation term is computed empirically from the batch. The phrasing could mislead a reader into thinking the correlation term itself is estimated via MC sampling, or it may simply be an imprecise shorthand. Clarifying which component requires the stochastic estimator is necessary for reproducibility.

comments.title: Correlation value exceeding theoretical bounds
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ .
comments.explanation: The text reports a correlation coefficient of 1.99. By definition, sample Pearson correlation coefficients are bounded within $[-1, 1]$. A value of 1.99 is mathematically impossible for a properly normalized correlation metric. This is almost certainly a typographical error for 0.99 (especially given the spacing in the OCR text `1 _._ 99`), but as written, it contradicts fundamental statistical definitions. The authors should verify and correct this value, as it affects the credibility of the reported performance.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Table 1 caption

comments.explanation: The main text and the table columns consistently refer to the true canonical vectors as $\phi$ and $\eta$. The table caption incorrectly refers to them as $\psi$ and $\eta$. This is a minor notation inconsistency that does not affect interpretation but should be corrected for consistency.

comments.explanation: The authors discuss constraints on the norms of transformed variables for both modalities $X$ and $Y$. However, the second condition incorrectly references $X$ again ($\|b^T X\|_2 < 1$) instead of $Y$. It should read $\|b^T Y\|_2 < 1$. This is a straightforward notation inconsistency that misattributes the constraint to the wrong view, though the intended meaning is recoverable from context.

comments.title: Ambiguous metric definition for scalar value > 1
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ .
comments.explanation: A standard correlation coefficient is mathematically bounded within $[-1, 1]$. The reported value of 1.99 exceeds this range. Given that the paragraph explicitly states the embedding dimension is set to $d=2$, this value almost certainly represents the *total correlation* (i.e., the sum or trace of the canonical correlations). Without explicit clarification, readers unfamiliar with DCCA's trace-based objective may misinterpret the metric or question the numerical validity of the result. Clarifying that this refers to the sum of canonical correlations would resolve the ambiguity.

comments.title: Typographical mismatch in table description
comments.quote: In Table 1 we present the estimated correlation along with the estimation errors of _**φ**_ and _**ρ**_ (averaged over 100 simulations).
comments.explanation: The table headers clearly list columns for $\hat{\rho}$, $E_\phi$, and $E_\eta$. The text mistakenly refers to the estimation error of $\rho$ instead of $\eta$. This is a minor typographical error that could cause a brief moment of confusion when cross-referencing the prose with the table.

comments.title: Unclear application of Monte Carlo sampling in loss computation
comments.quote: Specifically, we used Monte Carlo sampling to estimate the left part of Eq. 4. This is repeated for each batch, using one Monte Carlo sample per batch as suggested by Louizos et al. (2017) and Yamada et al. (2020), and worked well in our experiments.
comments.explanation: Equation 4 defines the loss as $\mathcal{L}(\theta, \mu) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{corr}}(\theta) + \lambda_x \mathbb{E}[\|\mathbf{z}_x\|_0] + \lambda_y \mathbb{E}[\|\mathbf{z}_y\|_0]$. The text states that Monte Carlo sampling estimates the 'left part' of Eq. 4. Typically, the correlation term $\mathcal{L}_{\text{corr}}$ is computed deterministically from the batch forward pass, while the MC sampling is applied to the expectation terms (the regularization gates) on the right. Phrasing it as the 'left part' is ambiguous and potentially backwards. It is unclear whether the authors mean the correlation term requires stochastic estimation (which would be unusual) or if they simply mislabeled the components. Clarifying which component uses single-sample MC estimation versus deterministic batch computation is necessary for reproducibility.

comments.title: Typographical error in SCCA variance condition
comments.quote: if _∥_ _**a[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1 or _∥_ _**b[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1, then the covariance in the objective is no longer equal to the correlation.
comments.explanation: When checking the condition under which covariance diverges from correlation in the SCCA objective, I initially noticed that the second term checks the norm of _**b**[^T]_ **X**. Given the context and the symmetry of the CCA formulation, this should clearly refer to the second modality's projection, _**b**[^T]_ **Y**. Leaving it as _**X**_ contradicts the established notation where _**X**_ and _**Y**_ are the two views, and would incorrectly imply checking the first modality twice. This is a straightforward typographical error that should be corrected to _**b**[^T]_ **Y**_ for mathematical consistency.

comments.title: Impossible canonical correlation value reported
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ .
comments.explanation: Canonical correlation is mathematically bounded by 1, as it is defined as the maximum correlation between linear combinations of the two views, which cannot exceed the bounds of the correlation coefficient. A reported value of 1.99 is physically impossible and indicates a significant reporting error. It is likely a typo for 0.99, or perhaps a different metric (e.g., a loss value or unnormalized inner product) was mistakenly labeled as correlation. This undermines the credibility of the numerical results in this section and should be verified and corrected.

comments.title: Incorrect metric referenced in text vs. Table 1

comments.explanation: The text claims to present estimation errors for _**φ**_ and _**ρ**_, but Table 1 explicitly lists columns for _E_**_φ**_ and _E_**_η**_. The symbol _**ρ**_ denotes the correlation coefficient, not a canonical vector, and no error metric _E_**_ρ**_ is defined or shown. The author clearly intended to refer to _**η**_ here. This mismatch between the prose and the presented table could mislead a careful reader tracking the experimental results.

comments.explanation: The caption for Table 1 refers to the canonical vectors _**ψ**_ and _**η**_, but the paper consistently uses _**φ**_ to denote the first canonical vector (e.g., in Section 3.1: 'canonical vectors _**φ**_ and _**η**_', and in the table itself: _E_**_φ**_). The symbol _**ψ**_ appears to be an OCR artifact or a typographical slip. progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This manuscript proposes Deep Gated-CCA, a method that addresses the practical limitations of existing Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) models, specifically regarding high-dimensional data where the number of variables exceeds the number of samples ($N < D$). The introduction clearly articulates the motivation, contrasting the scalability issues of kernel-based SCCA with the need for an end-to-end trainable deep model that incorporates sparse feature selection. The technical approach—integrating stochastic gates via a Gaussian relaxation to sparsify input variables while maximizing correlation—appears novel and well-motivated within the literature. The clarity of the writing is generally high, and the relationship to prior work (DCCA, SCCA) is well-established. The primary area of concern for the reviewer is the optimization dynamics; specifically, ensuring that the stochastic gate mechanism does not hinder convergence or degrade the quality of the extracted representations compared to standard sparse weight penalties. Furthermore, the claim of outperforming existing linear and non-linear CCA models (as stated in the abstract) warrants careful scrutiny regarding experimental design and comparison baselines.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Definitions
comments.quote:
The parameters of the networks are learned by applying stochastic gradient decent to the following objective:

where **θ_X** and **θ_Y** are the trainable parameters, and f(**X**) and g(**Y**) ∈ R^[d] are the desired correlated representations.
comments.explanation: Section 1.2 refers to 'the following objective' for DCCA, and Section 1.3 refers to 'the following regularized objective' for SCCA, but the equations defining these objectives are missing from the provided text. Additionally, Section 1.4 introduces the random variable **z** defined by a specific equation, but that equation is also absent. Without these formulas, it is impossible to verify the technical claims made in the surrounding prose (e.g., the bi-convexity properties or the specific regularization terms).

comments.title: Mathematical Impossibility in Reported Correlation
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1_._99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views **X** and **Y**.
comments.explanation: Section 3.2 reports a correlation value of 1.99 for the learned embeddings. Correlation coefficients (such as canonical correlation or Pearson correlation) are mathematically bounded between -1 and 1. A value of 1.99 is impossible and indicates either a typo (e.g., should be 0.99) or a confusion with a different metric (like total correlation). This directly undermines the claim of successful estimation of correlated representations.

comments.title: Ambiguous Notation in Model Specification
comments.quote: and Σ_XY = ρ Σ_X (φη^T) Σ_Y.
comments.explanation: In the linear model description (Section 3.1), the author specifies Σ_XY as ρ times Σ_X times (φη^T) times Σ_Y. Given that the text explicitly states Σ_X = Σ_Y = **I**^800 (the identity matrix), the terms Σ_X and Σ_Y effectively cancel out, simplifying the equation to ρ(φη^T). The explicit inclusion of the identity matrices in this context appears redundant and introduces unnecessary notation that could confuse a reader attempting to reconstruct the model.

comments.title: Underspecified Methodological Choices
comments.quote: using various values of λ = λx = λy.
comments.explanation: While Section 2.1 (The Model) defines the loss function with two distinct regularization parameters, λx and λy, corresponding to the two views, Section 3.1 simplifies this to a single scalar λ. Although this is a common experimental simplification, explicitly stating that the authors restrict themselves to the symmetric case (λx = λy) would improve reproducibility and clarify the methodological scope.

comments.title: Impossible Correlation Value
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1.99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views X and Y.
comments.explanation: The statement claims a correlation of 1.99. Correlation coefficients are mathematically bounded between -1 and 1. A value of 1.99 is physically impossible. The most likely explanation is a typographical error where '0.99' was intended (perhaps referring to the correlation squared, R²). This renders the specific quantitative claim in the text invalid.

comments.title: Notation Inconsistency with Introduced Symbols
comments.quote: In Table 1 we present the estimated correlation along with the estimation errors of \psi and \eta.
comments.explanation: The text immediately preceding this sentence introduces and discusses the true canonical vectors as \phi and \eta (e.g., "the canonical vectors \phi and \eta which maximize... are \phi and \eta"). The table itself labels the error metric for the first vector as $E_\phi$. The sudden appearance of $\psi$ without definition or mention is inconsistent with the context, likely a typo for $\phi$.

comments.title: Contradictory Objective Function (Max vs Min)
comments.quote: The Deep Gated CCA (DG-CCA) loss is defined by ... min_{\theta, \mu} ... Corr( f(z_x * X), g(z_y * Y) ) + ...
comments.explanation: Section 1.2 claims the goal is to "maximize the total correlation" between the neural networks' outputs. However, in Section 2.1, the loss function is explicitly written as a minimization of a 'total correlation term' ($\min \dots Corr(\cdot)$). Minimizing a positive quantity representing correlation directly contradicts the stated objective of maximizing it. It is likely the intended formula was $\min (-\text{Corr})$ or $\min (1 - \text{Corr})$.

comments.title: Unreadable Mathematical Notation in Figure Caption
comments.explanation: The LaTeX code rendering in the caption of Figure 3 is completely unintelligible due to severe OCR corruption. The formula attempts to define the estimated correlation (ρ-hat) using the estimated canonical vectors (phi-hat, eta-hat). The notation _**X** T_ and _**Y η**_ suggests a matrix-matrix-vector-vector product (X^T Y η-hat) or potentially subscripts for covariance matrices (Σ_X, Σ_Y), but without the underlying text, the equation is mathematically nonsensical. This makes it impossible to understand how the authors plotted the 'empirical correlation' in the left panel of Figure 3, which is critical to understanding the regularization path.

comments.title: Suspicious Dataset Size (Likely Typo)
comments.quote: Both views consists of 62_,_ 000 samples, of which we use 40_,_ 000 for training 12_,_ 000 for testing and 10 _,_ 000 are used as a validation set.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that the dataset is a variant of MNIST, noting that standard MNIST contains 60,000 training images (60K/10K). However, it claims to use 62,000 samples. The OCR output shows irregular characters like '62_,_' surrounding the comma, which strongly suggests a typo in the source text or OCR misinterpretation of '60,000' (perhaps an error in image cropping or text recognition). Using 62,000 samples without explanation introduces a reproducibility issue—if the intended value was 60,000, results might not be directly comparable to standard MNIST baselines or the authors' own implementation details.

comments.title: Ambiguous Metric in Table 2
comments.quote: In Table 2 we present the MI, _k_-means and SVM accuracies computed based on DG-CCA embedding.
comments.explanation: The table presents 'MI' (Mutual Information) values ranging from 0.001 to 2.05. Standard mutual information between two random variables is unbounded, but for classification tasks, normalized MI (clustering/prediction accuracy) is typically bounded between 0 and 1. A value of 2.05 on a 10-class MNIST problem implies a measure that is not standard Mutual Information or is extremely high, possibly the trace of the correlation matrix, a sum of correlation coefficients, or a different scoring metric. For seismic data (3 classes), the values are lower (0.001 - 0.97), yet clustering accuracy is extremely high (98%). The ambiguity in the 'MI' label makes it difficult for the reader to assess whether the 'high' scores for MNIST are comparable to the 'good' scores for Seismic in terms of normalized performance.

comments.title: Ambiguous evaluation protocol and dataset split
comments.quote: We omit 20% of the data as a validation set. Then we train DG-CCA to embed the data in 3 dimensions using several values for λ = λx = λy . Then, we use the model that attains maximal correlation on the validation set. In Table 2 we present the MI, k-means and SVM accuracies computed based on DG-CCA embedding.
comments.explanation: The authors describe omitting 20% of the data to create a validation set for selecting the regularization parameter λ. However, they do not explicitly state whether the metrics reported in Table 2 (SVM accuracy 97.2%, MI 0.97, etc.) were calculated on this validation set or on a separate held-out test set. If the results are from the validation set, and λ was selected to maximize performance on that set, the reported metrics represent an optimistic estimate of generalization (variance inflation). Furthermore, the paper does not specify the training split for DG-CCA (i.e., whether it trained on 100% of the data and only used the 20% for validation, or trained on 80%). In comparison, standard baselines like KCCA and DCCA typically train on the full dataset or a specific quarries split. The lack of clarity on the test set and the exact data split used for training and reporting makes the comparison in Table 2 difficult to verify and potentially unfair if baselines used different splits.

comments.title: Underspecified Methodological Choices
comments.quote: using various values of λ = λx = λy.
comments.explanation: While Section 2.1 (The Model) defines the loss function with two distinct regularization parameters, λx and λy, corresponding to the two views, Section 3.1 simplifies this to a single scalar λ. Although this is a common experimental simplification, explicitly stating that the authors restrict themselves to the symmetric case (λx = λy) would improve reproducibility and clarify the methodological scope.

comments.title: Impossible Correlation Value
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1.99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views X and Y.
comments.explanation: The statement claims a correlation of 1.99. Correlation coefficients are mathematically bounded between -1 and 1. A value of 1.99 is physically impossible. The most likely explanation is a typographical error where '0.99' was intended (perhaps referring to the correlation squared, R²). This renders the specific quantitative claim in the text invalid.

comments.title: Notation Inconsistency with Introduced Symbols
comments.quote: In Table 1 we present the estimated correlation along with the estimation errors of \psi and \eta.
comments.explanation: The text immediately preceding this sentence introduces and discusses the true canonical vectors as \phi and \eta (e.g., "the canonical vectors \phi and \eta which maximize... are \phi and \eta"). The table itself labels the error metric for the first vector as $E_\phi$. The sudden appearance of $\psi$ without definition or mention is inconsistent with the context, likely a typo for $\phi$.

comments.title: Contradictory Objective Function (Max vs Min)
comments.quote: The Deep Gated CCA (DG-CCA) loss is defined by ... min_{\theta, \mu} ... Corr( f(z_x * X), g(z_y * Y) ) + ...
comments.explanation: Section 1.2 claims the goal is to "maximize the total correlation" between the neural networks' outputs. However, in Section 2.1, the loss function is explicitly written as a minimization of a 'total correlation term' ($\min \dots Corr(\cdot)$). Minimizing a positive quantity representing correlation directly contradicts the stated objective of maximizing it. It is likely the intended formula was $\min (-\text{Corr})$ or $\min (1 - \text{Corr})$.

comments.title: Unreadable Mathematical Notation in Figure Caption
comments.explanation: The LaTeX code rendering in the caption of Figure 3 is completely unintelligible due to severe OCR corruption. The formula attempts to define the estimated correlation (ρ-hat) using the estimated canonical vectors (phi-hat, eta-hat). The notation _**X** T_ and _**Y η**_ suggests a matrix-matrix-vector-vector product (X^T Y η-hat) or potentially subscripts for covariance matrices (Σ_X, Σ_Y), but without the underlying text, the equation is mathematically nonsensical. This makes it impossible to understand how the authors plotted the 'empirical correlation' in the left panel of Figure 3, which is critical to understanding the regularization path.

comments.title: Suspicious Dataset Size (Likely Typo)
comments.quote: Both views consists of 62_,_ 000 samples, of which we use 40_,_ 000 for training 12_,_ 000 for testing and 10 _,_ 000 are used as a validation set.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that the dataset is a variant of MNIST, noting that standard MNIST contains 60,000 training images (60K/10K). However, it claims to use 62,000 samples. The OCR output shows irregular characters like '62_,_' surrounding the comma, which strongly suggests a typo in the source text or OCR misinterpretation of '60,000' (perhaps an error in image cropping or text recognition). Using 62,000 samples without explanation introduces a reproducibility issue—if the intended value was 60,000, results might not be directly comparable to standard MNIST baselines or the authors' own implementation details.

comments.title: Ambiguous Metric in Table 2
comments.quote: In Table 2 we present the MI, _k_-means and SVM accuracies computed based on DG-CCA embedding.
comments.explanation: The table presents 'MI' (Mutual Information) values ranging from 0.001 to 2.05. Standard mutual information between two random variables is unbounded, but for classification tasks, normalized MI (clustering/prediction accuracy) is typically bounded between 0 and 1. A value of 2.05 on a 10-class MNIST problem implies a measure that is not standard Mutual Information or is extremely high, possibly the trace of the correlation matrix, a sum of correlation coefficients, or a different scoring metric. For seismic data (3 classes), the values are lower (0.001 - 0.97), yet clustering accuracy is extremely high (98%). The ambiguity in the 'MI' label makes it difficult for the reader to assess whether the 'high' scores for MNIST are comparable to the 'good' scores for Seismic in terms of normalized performance.

comments.title: Ambiguous evaluation protocol and dataset split
comments.quote: We omit 20% of the data as a validation set. Then we train DG-CCA to embed the data in 3 dimensions using several values for λ = λx = λy . Then, we use the model that attains maximal correlation on the validation set. In Table 2 we present the MI, k-means and SVM accuracies computed based on DG-CCA embedding.
comments.explanation: The authors describe omitting 20% of the data to create a validation set for selecting the regularization parameter λ. However, they do not explicitly state whether the metrics reported in Table 2 (SVM accuracy 97.2%, MI 0.97, etc.) were calculated on this validation set or on a separate held-out test set. If the results are from the validation set, and λ was selected to maximize performance on that set, the reported metrics represent an optimistic estimate of generalization (variance inflation). Furthermore, the paper does not specify the training split for DG-CCA (i.e., whether it trained on 100% of the data and only used the 20% for validation, or trained on 80%). In comparison, standard baselines like KCCA and DCCA typically train on the full dataset or a specific quarries split. The lack of clarity on the test set and the exact data split used for training and reporting makes the comparison in Table 2 difficult to verify and potentially unfair if baselines used different splits.
